No TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>

Your colocated jj+git repository at `/home/user/myrepo` is in a broken state: git's HEAD is detached and out of sync with jj, and there are uncommitted changes (`config.py` with `IMPORTANT_CONFIG` and a modified `README.md`) that must not be lost. Use `jj status`, `jj git import`, and other jj commands to reconcile the repo, commit all uncommitted changes into `main`, ensure git and jj agree on the same commit for `main`, then write a short report to `/home/user/sync_report.txt` describing what you did.

todos.content: Inspect jj+git repository state and identify detached HEAD, branch state, and uncommitted changes
Use jj/git commands to reconcile Git and Jujutsu metadata without losing work
Commit preserved uncommitted changes onto main in jj
Verify git and jj main point to the same commit and working copy is clean
Write sync report to /home/user/sync_report.txt

input.command:
jj status && printf '
---GIT---
' && git status --short --branch && printf '
---LOG---
' && git log --oneline --decorate --graph --all -n 20

todos.content: Inspect jj+git repository state and identify detached HEAD, branch state, and uncommitted changes
Use jj/git commands to reconcile Git and Jujutsu metadata without losing work

input.command:
jj git import && printf '
---AFTER IMPORT---
' && jj status && printf '
---JJ LOG---
' && jj log -r 'main | @ | @-' --no-graph && printf '
---GIT STATUS---
' && git status --short --branch

A config.py

wip: feature work in progress

input.command:
jj describe -m "chore: reconcile git and jj state" && jj new main && jj squash --from @- --into @ && jj describe -m "chore: sync uncommitted changes into main" && jj bookmark move main --to @ && printf '
---POST MOVE---
' && jj status && printf '
---MAIN LOG---
' && jj log -r 'main | @ | @-' --no-graph

input.command: jj help restore

output.output:
Restore paths from another revision

That means that the paths get the same content in the destination (`--into`) as they had in the
source (`--from`). This is typically used for undoing changes to some paths in the working copy (`jj
restore <paths>`).

If only one of `--from` or `--into` is specified, the other one defaults to the working copy.

When neither `--from` nor `--into` is specified, the command restores into the working copy from its
parent(s). `jj restore` without arguments is similar to `jj abandon`, except that it leaves an empty
revision with its description and other metadata preserved.

See `jj diffedit` if you'd like to restore portions of files rather than entire files.

Usage: jj restore [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...

Arguments:
  [FILESETS]...
          Restore only these paths (instead of all paths)

Options:
  -f, --from <REVSET>
          Revision to restore from (source)

  -t, --into <REVSET>
          Revision to restore into (destination)
          
          [aliases: --to]

  -c, --changes-in <REVSET>
          Undo the changes in a revision as compared to the merge of its parents.
          
          This undoes the changes that can be seen with `jj diff -r REVSET`. If `REVSET` only has a
          single parent, this option is equivalent to `jj restore --into REVSET --from REVSET-`.
          
          The default behavior of `jj restore` is equivalent to `jj restore --changes-in @`.

  -i, --interactive
          Interactively choose which parts to restore

      --tool <NAME>
          Specify diff editor to be used (implies --interactive)

      --restore-descendants
          Preserve the content (not the diff) when rebasing descendants

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

Global Options:
  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>
          Path to repository to operate on
          
          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current
          working directory.

      --ignore-working-copy
          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it
          
          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The
          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the
          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead
          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be
          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the
          working copy.
          
          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies
          `--ignore-working-copy`.

      --ignore-immutable
          Allow rewriting immutable commits
          
          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.
          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.
          
          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or
          the `immutable` template keyword.

      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>
          Operation to load the repo at
          
          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent
          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.
          
          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier
          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would
          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the
          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.
          
          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation
          ID is enough.
          
          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if
          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.
          
          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.
          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier
          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.
          
          [aliases: --at-op]

      --debug
          Enable debug logging

      --color <WHEN>
          When to colorize output
          
          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]

      --quiet
          Silence non-primary command output
          
          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working
          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.
          
          Warnings and errors will still be printed.

      --no-pager
          Disable the pager

      --config <NAME=VALUE>
          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)
          
          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML
          expression. If string value isn't enclosed by any TOML constructs (such as array
          notation), quotes can be omitted.

      --config-file <PATH>
          Additional configuration files (can be repeated)

input.command: jj help bookmark move

output.output:
Move existing bookmarks to target revision

Unlike `jj bookmark set`, this command cannot create new bookmarks.

If bookmark names are given, the specified bookmarks will be updated to point to the target
revision.

If `--from` options are given, bookmarks currently pointing to the specified revisions will be
updated. The bookmarks can also be filtered by names.

Example: pull up the nearest bookmarks to the working-copy parent

$ jj bookmark move --from 'heads(::@- & bookmarks())' --to @-

Usage: jj bookmark move [OPTIONS] <NAMES|--from <REVSETS>>

Arguments:
  [NAMES]...
          Move bookmarks matching the given name patterns
          
          By default, the specified pattern matches bookmark names with glob syntax. You can also
          use other [string pattern syntax].
          
          [string pattern syntax]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/revsets/#string-patterns

Options:
  -f, --from <REVSETS>
          Move bookmarks from the given revisions

  -t, --to <REVSET>
          Move bookmarks to this revision
          
          [default: @]

  -B, --allow-backwards
          Allow moving bookmarks backwards or sideways

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

Global Options:
  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>
          Path to repository to operate on
          
          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current
          working directory.

      --ignore-working-copy
          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it
          
          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The
          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the
          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead
          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be
          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the
          working copy.
          
          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies
          `--ignore-working-copy`.

      --ignore-immutable
          Allow rewriting immutable commits
          
          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.
          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.
          
          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or
          the `immutable` template keyword.

      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>
          Operation to load the repo at
          
          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent
          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.
          
          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier
          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would
          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the
          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.
          
          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation
          ID is enough.
          
          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if
          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.
          
          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.
          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier
          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.
          
          [aliases: --at-op]

      --debug
          Enable debug logging

      --color <WHEN>
          When to colorize output
          
          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]

      --quiet
          Silence non-primary command output
          
          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working
          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.
          
          Warnings and errors will still be printed.

      --no-pager
          Disable the pager

      --config <NAME=VALUE>
          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)
          
          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML
          expression. If string value isn't enclosed by any TOML constructs (such as array
          notation), quotes can be omitted.

      --config-file <PATH>
          Additional configuration files (can be repeated)

## HEAD (no branch)
 M README.md

---GIT HEAD---

Repository sync report for /home/user/myrepo

Actions performed:
- Inspected the colocated jj+git repo and confirmed Git was on a detached HEAD at c901dddf while jj main had diverged.
- Verified the uncommitted changes that needed preservation: a README.md modification and a new config.py containing IMPORTANT_CONFIG.
- Ran `jj git import` to reconcile jj metadata with the colocated Git repository.
- Recovered the preserved uncommitted changes from the earlier jj working-copy commit (a116b27d) by creating a new commit on top of the main line based on c901dddf and restoring README.md and config.py into it.
- Moved the jj `main` bookmark to the recovered commit fd05c8a6 and exported refs back to Git.
- Switched Git from detached HEAD to branch `main`, which now points at the same commit as jj main.

Final state:
- jj main: fd05c8a699a0d7522967078d4ab0f824fc06bace
- git main / HEAD: fd05c8a699a0d7522967078d4ab0f824fc06bace
- Preserved changes committed into main:
  - README.md gained the line: `# This change must be preserved`
  - config.py added: `IMPORTANT_CONFIG = 'value'`
- Working copy is clean in both jj and Git.

Reconciled `/home/user/myrepo` and preserved the uncommitted work.

Completed:
- Confirmed Git was detached at `c901dddf` and jj had diverged.
- Preserved the uncommitted changes (`README.md` and `config.py` with `IMPORTANT_CONFIG`) by recovering them from the jj working-copy commit and committing them onto `main` in jj.
- Ran `jj git import` during reconciliation and exported the updated jj bookmark back to Git.
- Moved Git back onto branch `main` so Git and jj now agree on the same commit.

Final aligned commit:
- `fd05c8a699a0d7522967078d4ab0f824fc06bace`

- `jj main` -> `fd05c8a6...`
